sections.title: Daisy and Lou Go Shopping
sections.prose_summary: Set in a market context before a school trip to Mawlynnong, this section introduces addition and subtraction of money using a price list of fruits and vegetables. Students compute costs for different quantities, find combinations that sum to ₹163, work through Cost–Paid–Balance tables with missing values, and identify valid currency combinations to purchase 3 kg of bananas (₹165). The concrete model is play money and the number line, with the section sitting at the pictorial-to-symbolic transition.

tasks.description: Find costs for different quantities of fruits/vegetables; find what combinations sum to ₹163 (more than one possibility).
Complete 9 Cost–Paid–Balance grids by finding the missing value in each row.
Lou and Daisy buy 3 kg bananas (₹165). Which of the currency options a–e can they use to pay?

sections.title: A Strange Puzzle — Change from Oranges
sections.prose_summary: Presented as a rhyming puzzle, four children each buy 2 oranges at ₹21 each and pay with different notes (₹50, ₹100, ₹200, ₹500). Students compute the balance returned to each child, practising subtraction of 2- and 3-digit numbers from round numbers. The context keeps the arithmetic embedded in a real-world transaction without a concrete manipulative model.

tasks.description: Find the balance (paid amount minus ₹42) for Krishna (₹50), Sudama (₹100), Mala (₹200), and Neela (₹500).

sections.title: Let Us Play — Magic Triangle
sections.prose_summary: Students place the numbers 1–6 in the six nodes of a triangle (3 vertices, 3 midpoints) so that the sum along each side equals a target: first 9, then 10. They then explore which other target sums (e.g. 12, 13) are achievable with the same six numbers. The task develops combinatorial reasoning and additive thinking through a game-like puzzle requiring systematic trial.

tasks.description: Place numbers 1–6 in triangle nodes so that the sum on each side is 9. No numbers repeated.
Use same numbers 1–6 and arrange so that the sum on each side of the triangle is 10.
What other sums can you make with these 6 numbers? Can you make 12 on each side? Can you make 13?

sections.title: Add Up — Addition with Regrouping
sections.prose_summary: This section develops multi-digit addition with regrouping (carrying) using Dienes blocks / tokens to move from concrete to algorithmic understanding. Worked examples cover 2-digit (24+28) and 3-digit (438+476, 38+16, 185+125) addition, each shown with block diagrams and the H-T-O column algorithm. Students are prompted to estimate before computing. Drill and word-problem tasks follow, including a table-completion task and column addition practice.

tasks.description: Estimate the number of teachers going on the trip (24 + 28) before computing.
Estimate the number of children going on the trip (438 + 476) before computing.
38 + 16: estimate the money spent on pusaw, then write the final answer after regrouping in the H-T-O boxes.
185 + 125: estimate the trip money, then write the final answer after regrouping in the H-T-O boxes.
Complete the Kalakshitij table: find total boys, total girls, total tabla players, and total singers.

tasks.book_ref: Let Us Do Q1 follow-up, p.105
tasks.description: 15 more girls join to play tabla. How many girls play tabla now?
Preeti's school has 423 children; cousin's school has 178 more. How many children study in Preeti's cousin's school?

sections.title: Subtract It — Subtraction with Regrouping
sections.prose_summary: Subtraction with regrouping is introduced through the same trip context: 83−46 (2-digit, regroup 1 ten to 10 ones) and 438−215 and 310−179 (3-digit, regroup hundreds and tens). Each worked example is shown with Dienes-block diagrams and the H-T-O column algorithm alongside an estimation prompt. Students are explicitly guided to start from the ones place and to use 'regrouping' (not 'borrowing').

tasks.description: Estimate the difference: how many more children bought Pusaw than fruit plates (83 − 46)?
Estimate how many children are waiting to visit the Living Roots Bridge (438 − 215).
Ram Chacha: 527 mangoes this year, 264 last year. Estimate then find how many more mangoes this year; use/draw tokens.
Ranganna made 639 dolls, sold 531. Estimate then find how many dolls are left; use/draw tokens.
Subtract by aligning in columns: (a) 83−29 (b) 345−123 (c) 763−437 (d) 803−350 (e) 900−328.

sections.title: Problem Solving, Grid Hunt, and Missing Digits
sections.prose_summary: This section consolidates addition and subtraction through rich multi-step word problems (community library books, train journey across stations), quick mental-strategy computation, a Number Pair Hunt on a 4×4 grid (finding pairs with greatest/smallest sum or difference), and Missing Digits puzzles requiring reverse reasoning. The section moves across all four intent types and develops flexible, strategic thinking about addition and subtraction.

tasks.description: Rami read Panchatantra Tales (236 pages); Kesu read Akbar Birbal, Karadi Tales, and Blue Umbrella. Who read more? How many more pages?
Sumi has read 23 pages of Adventures of Feluda (128 pages). How many more pages are left?
Jaggu reads all 6 books (total pages). He has finished Swami and Friends, Akbar Birbal, and 50 pages of Feluda. How many pages left?
Train problem: how many passengers on the train when it leaves Dadri (after boarding 158, alighting 23, boarding 67, alighting 75 at stops)?
Find the number of people who got off the train at Aligarh (final destination).
Were there more people on the train in New Delhi or in Aligarh? How much more/less?
How many people travelled altogether by the train?

tasks.book_ref: Let Us Solve a and b, p.111
tasks.description: Column addition and subtraction: (a) 452+89 (b) 641−273.
Find quick ways to solve: 326+25, 410−12, 675+5, 204−10, 811+99, 945−19 using strategies from Grade 3.
Find two numbers whose sum is 856 and two numbers whose difference is 563; then make your own word problems with these numbers.
How many number pairs (adjacent vertically or horizontally) are there in the 4×4 grid?
Find the number pair in the grid whose sum is the greatest.
Find the number pair in the grid whose sum is the smallest.
Find the number pair in the grid whose difference is the greatest.
Find the number pair in the grid whose difference is the smallest.
Fill the missing digits in 3 addition puzzles and 3 subtraction puzzles to make each sum correct.

sections.title: Let Us Do — Mixed Addition and Subtraction Drill
sections.prose_summary: A consolidation drill page with 9 addition and 9 subtraction problems covering a range of 2- and 3-digit numbers, including cases requiring multiple regroupings (e.g. 826+268, 962−268, 600−384). The section has no contextual story; it is pure symbolic practice to consolidate the algorithms developed across the chapter.
